questionText: Differentiate $y = \ln x$ with respect to $x$, stating any restriction on $x$.

steps.description: Identify the function and the goal
steps.explanation: We want $\frac{dy}{dx}$ for the natural logarithm. Note $\ln x$ is only defined for $x>0$, so that restriction carries through.

steps.description: Recognise this as a new standard result
steps.workingLatex: y = \ln x \quad (\text{a standard derivative to learn})
steps.explanation: Unlike a power of $x$, the logarithm cannot be differentiated with the power rule; its derivative is a separate standard result worth memorising.

steps.description: Recall the standard derivative of the natural logarithm
steps.explanation: The derivative of $\ln x$ is the standard result $\tfrac{1}{x}$; this is one of the key new results to memorise.
So the gradient function of $y=\ln x$ is simply $\tfrac{1}{x}$.
For every allowed $x$ the gradient is positive, so $\ln x$ is always increasing, but the slope shrinks as $x$ grows.
The derivative inherits the domain of the original function, so we keep the condition $x>0$.
